decision_summary: The chip peak temperature (89.26°C) exceeds the limit (85.0°C). The most effective primary lever is to increase the heat spreader conductivity, which is currently at a moderate level (500 W/mK) but has room to grow within bounds. Increasing conductivity by a factor of 1.5 (to 750 W/mK) will significantly improve lateral heat spreading, reducing the local hot spot temperature.

changes.reason: Increasing spreader conductivity enhances lateral heat diffusion away from the chip, directly lowering the peak temperature. This change respects the 2.0x single-step ratio limit.

expected_effects: More uniform temperature distribution across the heat spreader
Improved thermal coupling between the chip and the boundary conditions
risk_notes: Diminishing returns may occur if the bottleneck shifts to the base plate or boundary convection rather than lateral spreading.
High conductivity materials may be cost-prohibitive in real-world applications, though this is a simulation constraint check.
